title: Halászlé
subtitle: Hungarian fisherman's paprika soup
excerpt: A vivid red fish soup built on a base of onion and sweet paprika, with chunks of white fish simmered directly in the peppery broth. One of Hungary's most intensely flavoured dishes, minimal ingredients, maximum colour.
sourceNotes: Halászlé (fisherman's soup) is one of the defining dishes of Hungarian cuisine, traditionally made by Danube and Tisza river fishermen using carp, pike-perch, or catfish. The soup is distinguished by its extreme simplicity: fish, water, onion, and a generous quantity of sweet and hot paprika, and by the deep red colour and intensity of the paprika broth. The fish stock used as the base is ideally made from the fish heads and bones cooked separately; this version uses a light chicken stock as a practical shortcut that does not compromise the paprika flavour.

content.text: Halászlé has almost no ingredients: onion, paprika, fish, and water. The intensity of the finished soup depends entirely on the quality and quantity of the paprika and on not overcooking the fish. Hungarian sweet paprika should be used here, not smoked, not hot as the dominant seasoning, but sweet and full-flavoured. A small amount of hot paprika sharpens the broth without dominating it.

The paprika base is cooked out for 20 minutes before the fish goes in. This is not a step to rush. The onion and paprika need time to form a coherent broth; the fish needs only 8 to 10 minutes and must not be overcooked.

Warm the {{sunflower-oil}} in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 10 minutes until soft and beginning to colour. Remove from the heat, this is important; do not add paprika to a pan on the heat or it burns. Stir in the {{paprika-sweet}} and {{paprika-hot}}, then return to the heat and immediately pour in the {{stock-chicken}}. Add the {{tomato}} and {{pepper-green}}. Bring to a simmer and cook for 20 minutes.

Season the broth with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Taste. It should be intensely paprika-flavoured and savoury. Gently add the {{cod-fillet}} and simmer for 8 to 10 minutes until the fish is just cooked through. Do not stir: move the pot gently if needed. Ladle into deep bowls with the fish and vegetables. Serve with white bread.

items.fix: Halászlé uses three tablespoons of paprika for four portions, this is not a mistake. Use the best Hungarian sweet paprika available. If the broth tastes thin, simmer uncovered for 10 minutes to concentrate
items.cause: Too little paprika, or poor-quality paprika

items.fix: Paprika burns in seconds in hot oil. Always remove the pan from the heat before adding paprika, then immediately add liquid to stop the cooking
items.cause: Added to hot oil rather than off the heat

items.fix: Do not stir once the fish is in the pot. Simmer gently and check after 8 minutes by pressing a piece. It should flake but hold its shape
items.symptom: Fish falling apart in the broth

content.text: Halászlé is most associated with the great Hungarian rivers (the Danube and Tisza) and with the fishing towns of Baja and Szeged, each of which claims the definitive version. The Baja version cooks the fish and pasta together in the same pot; the Szeged version keeps them separate. Both are made outdoors in a large cauldron over an open fire at festivals and celebrations. At home, it is a winter dish: warming, direct, and made with whatever firm white river fish is available.
